I find it fascinating that people used to believe the Sun orbits the Earth. It's amazing how a simple observation, like the Sun rising in the East, can lead to a misconception about the entire universe. I'm intrigued by the idea that the Earth is actually a satellite of the Sun, and that there are many other satellites orbiting around it. I'm also curious about the concept of gravity and how it controls the orbits of planets and moons. The article mentions that the planets don't move in perfect ...

I find it fascinating how Sir Isaac Newton's work dominated physics for centuries. His contributions to the laws of motion, optics, gravity, and calculus are truly groundbreaking. I'm intrigued by the fact that he developed a theory of universal gravitation and three laws of motion. The idea that he built the first practical reflecting telescope in 1668 is also remarkable. What I find most interesting, however, is the collaborative aspect of his work, specifically his shared credit with Gottfrie...

I find it fascinating that calculus was discovered independently by two men, Newton and Leibniz, in the 17th century. The fact that they worked separately from each other and yet arrived at the same conclusions is a testament to the power of human ingenuity. I'm also intrigued by the idea that calculus is a tool for describing continuous change, which seems to be a fundamental aspect of the natural world.
